title: 0/1 Knapsack
description: The classic 'include or exclude' 2D DP problem -- choosing items with weights and values to maximize value under a weight limit, each item usable at most once.
objectives: Understand the 0/1 knapsack problem and why greedy fails on it
Define dp[i][w] and derive its include/exclude recurrence
Trace the table to see how the optimal selection emerges

sections.text: Given items, each with a weight and a value, and a knapsack with a maximum weight capacity, choose a subset of items to MAXIMIZE total value without exceeding the capacity. Each item can be taken ONCE or NOT AT ALL -- hence '0/1'.

Why not just Greedy Algorithms? A greedy rule like 'always take the highest-value item first' or 'best value-per-weight ratio first' can fail here -- taking a slightly less efficient combination of smaller items sometimes beats one very efficient large item, since capacity is a hard constraint. This is a case Greedy genuinely cannot solve correctly in general.
The maximum value achievable using only the first i items, with a total weight capacity of w.

dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w] handles 'don't take item i-1' (the value stays whatever it was without this item). The second line handles 'take item i-1' -- ONLY possible if weights[i-1] <= w (it fits), giving values[i-1] PLUS whatever was achievable with the remaining capacity (w - weights[i-1]) using only EARLIER items. Math.max picks whichever option is better.

sections.title: Tracing key cells for weights={1,3,4,5}, values={1,4,5,7}, capacity=7
steps: Row i=1 (item0: weight1,value1): dp[1][w] = 1 for all w>=1 (it always fits and is worth taking), 0 for w=0
Row i=4 (item3: weight5,value7): dp[4][7] = max(dp[3][7], dp[3][2]+7)
Final answer dp[4][7] = 9 -- achieved by taking items with weight 3 (value 4) and weight 4 (value 5): total weight 7, total value 9

sections.title: Checking dp[4][7] = 9 by hand
points: Taking items (3,4) and (4,5): total weight 3+4=7 (exactly fits), total value 4+5=9
Taking item (5,7) alone plus item (1,1): total weight 5+1=6, total value 7+1=8 -- worse than 9
9 is indeed the best combination fitting within capacity 7

sections.text: Notice the greedy 'best value first' rule would grab item (5,7) first (highest value) -- leaving only capacity 2, which can only fit item (1,1) for a total of 8, WORSE than the true optimal 9. This concretely demonstrates greedy's failure on 0/1 Knapsack, exactly as warned in Greedy Algorithms.

The state genuinely needs TWO dimensions -- WHICH items have been considered so far (i), AND how much capacity REMAINS (w) -- neither dimension alone captures enough information to make the next decision. This is exactly the 2D DP pattern from the 2D DP lesson, just with a fundamentally different recurrence (include/exclude) than the grid-traversal problems there.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to solve 0/1 Knapsack with a greedy 'best value' or 'best ratio' rule -- it can produce a suboptimal answer, as shown in this lesson's own example.
Forgetting the weights[i-1] <= w check before considering 'take item i-1' -- attempting to include an item heavier than the remaining capacity is invalid.
Mixing up the 0-indexed weights/values arrays with the 1-indexed dp table -- dp row i corresponds to item i-1 in the original arrays.
Forgetting dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w] must be set FIRST (the 'exclude' option), before conditionally improving it with the 'include' option.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does dp[i][w] represent in the 0/1 Knapsack DP solution?
questions.answer: The maximum value achievable using only the first i items, with total weight capacity w.

questions.question: What is the recurrence for 0/1 Knapsack, and what do its two parts represent?
questions.answer: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weights[i-1]] + values[i-1]) if weights[i-1] <= w, else just dp[i-1][w]. The first term represents excluding item i-1; the second represents including it, adding its value to whatever was achievable with the remaining capacity using earlier items.

questions.question: Why does a greedy 'take the highest value item first' strategy fail on 0/1 Knapsack?
questions.answer: Taking the highest-value item can consume capacity that would have been better used by a combination of smaller items -- a concrete example shows greedy achieving 8 while the true optimal (from DP) achieves 9, since capacity is a hard constraint that greedy doesn't account for globally.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: 0/1 Knapsack defines dp[i][w] as the maximum value achievable using only the first i items within weight capacity w, with the recurrence dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w] [exclude item i-1], dp[i-1][w-weights[i-1]] + values[i-1] [include it, if it fits]). This needs a genuine 2D state, since both which items have been considered and how much capacity remains matter simultaneously. Greedy strategies (like always taking the highest-value item) provably fail here -- a concrete example shows greedy achieving a worse total than DP's guaranteed-optimal answer, because capacity is a hard constraint that local greedy choices don't respect globally.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: 0/1 Knapsack: at every item, ask 'skip it, or take it (if it fits) and keep the rest of the capacity for the earlier items to fight over?' Take whichever total wins. Greedy grabs the shiniest item first and regrets it later -- DP checks every combination properly.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What does dp[i][w] represent in 0/1 Knapsack?
What are the two options considered at each cell, and which condition gates one of them?
Why does a greedy highest-value-first strategy fail on this problem?
Why does 0/1 Knapsack need a 2D state instead of 1D?
